description: A rare and majestic Southern California endemic oak that supports over 300 species of wildlife. Its acorns are a critical food source for Acorn Woodpeckers, Western Scrub-Jays, and mule deer, while its canopy provides nesting habitat for raptors and songbirds. Listed as endangered due to habitat loss, making backyard cultivation an act of conservation Its wind-pollinated catkins also attract small beetles and native bees collecting pollen in spring.

image.attribution: Photo by Fred Roberts, CC BY-NC 4.0

plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Clay, loam, or sandy; tolerates poor soils; no amendments needed

maintenance.wateringNotes: Deep water monthly in summer for first 2–3 years; no irrigation once established. Overwatering causes root rot.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Minimal pruning; remove only dead or crossing branches in late summer. Never prune in wet season to avoid oak disease.
maintenance.specialNotes: Protect root zone from compaction and grade changes. Do not irrigate within drip line of mature trees.

phenology.ecologicalValue: Acorns feed Acorn Woodpeckers, Scrub-Jays, Band-tailed Pigeons, squirrels, and deer. Catkins provide early-season pollen for native bees.

wildlife.notes: Harvests and caches acorns in granary trees

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Nests in canopy; buries acorns for winter food caches

image.attribution: Photo by David Bygott, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Larvae feed exclusively on oaks; adults often seen gliding around canopy

image.attribution: Photo by Alice Abela, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: The iconic shrub that gave Hollywood its name. Toyon produces abundant bright red berries in winter when little else is fruiting, making it critical for overwintering birds like Cedar Waxwings, American Robins, and Hermit Thrushes. Its summer flowers feed native bees and butterflies.

image.attribution: Photo by Ken-ichi Ueda, CC BY-NC 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Tolerates steep slopes; excellent for erosion control
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Clay, loam, or sandy; very adaptable; no amendments

maintenance.wateringNotes: Occasional deep water in summer for first 1–2 years. Fully drought-tolerant once established.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune to shape in late winter after berries are consumed. Can be hedged or left natural.
maintenance.specialNotes: Fire-follower — resprouts vigorously after fire. May get fireblight in humid conditions; prune affected branches.

berry.description: Bright red berries persist through winter

phenology.ecologicalValue: Summer flowers feed native bees; winter berries are critical food for Cedar Waxwings, Robins, Mockingbirds, and Hermit Thrushes.

wildlife.notes: Flocks descend on berry-laden plants in winter

image.attribution: Photo by Kyle Nessen, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Major winter food source for overwintering robins

image.attribution: Photo by Brian Henderson, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Nests in cavities near Toyon; feeds berries to fledglings

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A fast-growing evergreen shrub with distinctive taco-shaped leaves that indicate frost-free zones. Its dense canopy provides essential shelter and nesting habitat for birds year-round, while its small fruits feed numerous bird species in late summer and fall.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on slopes; deep roots prevent erosion
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Any well-drained soil; tolerates clay and rocky soil

maintenance.wateringNotes: No irrigation once established. One of the most drought-tolerant native shrubs.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune to shape in late fall. Can be cut back hard and will resprout from base.
maintenance.specialNotes: Frost-sensitive; the folded leaves are a reliable indicator of frost-free microclimates. Vigorous resprouter after fire.

berry.description: Small white drupes in dense clusters

phenology.ecologicalValue: Flowers attract native bees and butterflies; fruits eaten by mockingbirds, thrashers, and other birds; dense canopy provides critical nesting cover.

wildlife.notes: Important nectar source during migration years

image.attribution: Photo by Anne Toal, CC BY 4.0

wildlife.notes: Feeds on drupes in late summer; nests in dense interior branches

image.attribution: Photo by Alan Vernon, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Dense canopy provides important nesting cover for this threatened species

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A tough, broom-like shrub that thrives in disturbed areas and provides late-season nectar when few other plants are blooming. Its cottony seed heads in fall are a distinctive sight, and the dense branches offer year-round shelter for lizards and small birds.

image.attribution: Photo by Jay Keller, CC BY-NC 4.0

plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy, loam, or rocky; very tolerant of poor soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Extremely drought-tolerant. No supplemental water once established.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Can be pruned hard in late winter to maintain shape. Female plants produce copious seed fluff — prune seed heads if unwanted.
maintenance.specialNotes: Dioecious — plant male plants if seed fluff is a concern. Can spread aggressively in irrigated gardens.

seed.description: Cottony seed heads disperse on wind

phenology.ecologicalValue: Critical late-season nectar for migrating butterflies and native bees. One of the few plants blooming in fall when pollinators need fuel.

wildlife.notes: Important fall nectar source for migrating Monarchs

image.attribution: Photo by Andrew Cannizzaro, CC BY 4.0

wildlife.notes: Dense broom-like branches provide year-round cover

image.attribution: Photo by Robert Bhatt, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A striking manzanita with smooth red bark and leathery bicolored leaves — dark green above, white below. Endemic to San Diego County's chaparral, it provides early-season nectar for hummingbirds and bees when few other plants are blooming, and its berries feed birds through summer.

image.attribution: Photo by Belinda Lo, CC BY-NC 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on slopes; needs sharp drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy or rocky, well-drained; no clay; no amendments

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer water once established — overwatering is fatal. One of the most drought-tolerant manzanitas.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Rarely needs pruning. Remove only dead wood. Do not cut into old wood — it won't resprout.
maintenance.specialNotes: Extremely sensitive to summer irrigation. Site on a well-drained slope away from irrigated areas. Slow-growing but very long-lived.

berry.description: Small manzanita berries ripen through summer

phenology.ecologicalValue: Critical winter/early spring nectar source for Anna's Hummingbirds and overwintering bumblebees. Berries feed Wrentits, thrashers, and coyotes.

wildlife.notes: Primary winter nectar source; hummingbirds defend individual plants

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Early blooms critical for this endangered bumblebee species emerging from hibernation

image.attribution: Photo by Ken-ichi Ueda, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: A chaparral specialist that feeds on manzanita berries and nests in dense shrubs

image.attribution: Photo by Alan Vernon, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A cheerful, long-blooming shrub that produces abundant daisy-like yellow flowers from spring through summer. It's a pollinator magnet that attracts dozens of native bee species, butterflies, and beneficial insects. Easy to grow and quick to establish, making it an ideal starter plant for new native gardens.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Flat to moderate slopes; tolerates coastal exposure
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; well-drained; tolerates poor soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Drought-deciduous — drops leaves in summer drought, which is normal. Occasional spring water extends bloom period.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back by half in late fall to maintain compact shape and prevent legginess. Deadhead spent flowers to extend blooming.
maintenance.specialNotes: Short-lived (3–5 years) but self-seeds readily. Replace old plants as they decline.

seed.description: Small achenes in dried flower heads; eaten by finches

phenology.ecologicalValue: One of the best native pollinator plants — attracts 50+ species of native bees, painted lady butterflies, and hover flies. Seeds feed Lesser Goldfinches.

wildlife.notes: Frequent visitor during spring migration

image.attribution: Photo by Anne Toal, CC BY 4.0

wildlife.notes: Flocks feed on dried seed heads in summer and fall

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: The signature aromatic shrub of coastal sage scrub, California Sagebrush defines the plant community that once covered much of Southern California. Its fragrant foliage is a critical host plant for several butterfly species and provides year-round cover for California Gnatcatchers and other declining sage scrub birds Though wind-pollinated, its small flower heads also attract native bees collecting pollen in fall.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on dry slopes; good for erosion control
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; well-drained; no amendments; tolerates poor soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental water once established. Drought-deciduous — drops leaves in summer, re-leafs with fall rains.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Light pruning in fall to maintain shape. Can be cut back to 12 inches to rejuvenate.
maintenance.specialNotes: Allelopathic — may inhibit some plants nearby. Highly aromatic foliage. Important Chumash and Kumeyaay medicinal plant.

phenology.ecologicalValue: Defines coastal sage scrub habitat. Host plant for California Sagebrush moth. Foliage provides year-round cover for threatened California Gnatcatcher.

wildlife.notes: This threatened species depends on sagebrush-dominated habitat for foraging and nesting

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Larvae feed inside the stems

description: The single most important native pollinator plant in Southern California. California Buckwheat supports over 50 species of native bees and is visited by countless butterflies. Its flowers transition from white to pink to rust as they age, providing nectar from spring through fall, and the dried seed heads feed birds through winter.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Well-drained; excellent on slopes and road cuts
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam, low fertility, no amendments needed

maintenance.wateringNotes: Extremely drought-tolerant once established; no summer water needed.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back by 1/3 in late fall after seeds drop to maintain compact form.
maintenance.specialNotes: Leave dried flower/seed heads through winter for bird foraging. One of the easiest natives to grow.

seed.description: Russet seed heads persist through winter, gradually consumed by finches

phenology.ecologicalValue: Major nectar source for 50+ native bee species, Painted Ladies, hairstreak butterflies. Seeds eaten by goldfinches and sparrows.

wildlife.notes: Buckwheat is a primary host plant for this common blue butterfly

image.attribution: Photo by Bill Bouton, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Flocks feed on dried seed heads through winter

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: Sacred to Indigenous peoples and beloved by pollinators, White Sage produces tall flowering spikes that are a magnet for native bees, especially large carpenter bees and bumblebees. Its intensely aromatic silver-white foliage is deer-resistant and the plant is supremely drought-tolerant once established.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Well-drained slopes preferred; avoid low spots where water pools
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; excellent drainage essential; no clay

maintenance.wateringNotes: No summer water once established — overwatering causes root rot and death.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Remove spent flower stalks after bloom. Light shaping in fall if needed.
maintenance.specialNotes: Culturally significant to Kumeyaay and other Indigenous peoples. Avoid harvesting from wild populations. Deer-resistant.

seed.description: Nutlets in persistent calyces along tall stalks

phenology.ecologicalValue: Major bee plant — visited by carpenter bees, bumblebees, and honey bees. Hummingbirds visit flowers. Seeds eaten by sparrows and juncos.

wildlife.notes: Large golden females are the primary pollinators, buzz-pollinating the flowers

image.attribution: Photo by Lon Brehmer, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Visits flower spikes; males display nearby during breeding season

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Arriving winter flocks forage on fallen seeds

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: The 'honey sage' — named for the exceptional quality of honey produced from its nectar. Black Sage is the backbone of coastal sage scrub alongside California Sagebrush, supporting an extraordinary diversity of native bees. Its whorled flower clusters bloom in a sequence up the stalk, providing extended nectar availability.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Slopes and flats; needs good drainage
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; well-drained; tolerates poor soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Drought-deciduous — drops leaves in summer. No summer water once established.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back by 1/3 after bloom to maintain shape. Avoid cutting into old wood.
maintenance.specialNotes: Highly aromatic. Produces some of California's finest sage honey. Deer-resistant.

seed.description: Nutlets in persistent whorled calyces; important for seed-eating birds

phenology.ecologicalValue: Premier native bee plant. Whorled flower clusters provide sequential blooming up each stalk. Important nectar source for butterflies and hummingbirds.

wildlife.notes: Regular visitor throughout bloom period

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Scratches beneath plants for fallen seeds

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A hummingbird favorite with tubular red-orange flowers that bloom over a long season. Endemic to San Diego County and northern Baja California, Red Bush Monkeyflower is perfectly adapted to the local climate and provides critical nectar for resident and migrating hummingbirds.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; well-drained; no clay; tolerates rocky soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Occasional deep water in spring extends bloom. Reduce to none in late summer.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back by half after main bloom flush in early summer. Promotes fall rebloom.
maintenance.specialNotes: May go dormant in summer drought. Light afternoon shade in hottest inland areas helps extend bloom.

seed.description: Small capsules split to release tiny seeds

phenology.ecologicalValue: Primary hummingbird nectar plant. Tubular flowers exclude most insects, channeling nectar to hummingbirds. Also visited by carpenter bees that pierce the flower base.

wildlife.notes: Primary pollinator; tubular flowers are perfectly shaped for hummingbird bills

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Spring migrant stops to refuel on monkeyflower nectar

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Monkeyflowers are a host plant for Common Buckeye butterfly larvae

image.attribution: Photo by Bill Bouton, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: The ultimate fall hummingbird plant — California Fuchsia explodes with brilliant scarlet tubular flowers from late summer into fall, precisely when hummingbirds are fattening up for migration or establishing winter territories. One of the very few native plants that provides abundant nectar in the dry fall months.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Slopes or flat; good drainage preferred
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; adaptable; tolerates rocky soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: Occasional summer water keeps it greener and extends bloom. Tolerates drought but blooms better with some water.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter after frost. New growth emerges from base in spring.
maintenance.specialNotes: Spreads by underground rhizomes — can be aggressive. Plant where spreading is welcome or contain with hardscape.

seed.description: Small parachute-like seeds dispersed by wind

phenology.ecologicalValue: The most important fall hummingbird plant in coastal sage scrub. Fills the critical nectar gap between summer-blooming sages and winter-blooming manzanitas.

wildlife.notes: Resident hummingbirds depend on this plant as summer flowers fade

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Fall migrants fuel up on California Fuchsia during southward migration

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Visits flowers at dusk; caterpillars also feed on Epilobium foliage

image.attribution: Photo by Ken-ichi Ueda, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A cheerful low-growing perennial that covers itself in clusters of small golden flowers in spring. Golden Yarrow is an excellent groundcover for dry slopes and its flowers attract a wide variety of small native bees, hover flies, and butterflies. Its woolly silver foliage is attractive year-round.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on dry slopes and banks
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; well-drained; tolerates rocky and poor soils

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental water once established. Extremely drought-tolerant.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Shear lightly after bloom to maintain compact form and promote denser growth.
maintenance.specialNotes: Semi-evergreen — may lose some leaves in extreme drought. Self-seeds in favorable conditions.

seed.description: Tiny achenes in dried flower clusters

phenology.ecologicalValue: Important nectar source for small native bees and hover flies. Attracts beneficial predatory insects that control garden pests.

wildlife.notes: Visits dried flower heads for seeds

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: The native milkweed essential for Monarch butterfly survival in Southern California. Narrowleaf Milkweed is the exclusive larval food plant for Monarch caterpillars and provides nectar for adult butterflies, native bees, and many other pollinators. Planting native milkweed (not tropical) is one of the most impactful things a gardener can do for Monarch conservation.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Flat to gentle slopes; tolerates seasonal flooding
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Loam to clay; adaptable; tolerates heavier soils than most natives

maintenance.wateringNotes: Moderate summer water extends the growing season and supports more caterpillar broods. More water-tolerant than most natives.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Cut back to ground in late fall after stems die back naturally. Do NOT cut while caterpillars are present — check carefully first.
maintenance.specialNotes: Goes fully dormant in winter — mark location to avoid accidental disturbance. Spreads by rhizomes. CRITICAL: use only native milkweed, never tropical milkweed (A. curassavica) which disrupts Monarch migration.

seed.description: Large pods split to release silky-parachuted seeds

phenology.ecologicalValue: THE essential Monarch butterfly host plant. Also critical nectar source for native bees, other butterflies, and beneficial insects like Milkweed Bugs and Milkweed Beetles.

wildlife.notes: Monarch caterpillars exclusively feed on milkweed — the only larval host plant. Multiple broods per year.

image.attribution: Photo by Andrew Cannizzaro, CC BY 4.0

wildlife.notes: Feeds on milkweed seeds; distinctive orange and black coloring warns predators

image.attribution: Photo by Ken-ichi Ueda, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A nitrogen-fixing pioneer plant that rapidly colonizes disturbed ground and improves soil for other natives. Deerweed's small yellow flowers bloom prolifically and are critical for native bees, while the plant is one of the primary host plants for several blue butterfly species including the endangered Palos Verdes Blue.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on disturbed slopes and road cuts
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; poor soils fine — fixes its own nitrogen; well-drained

maintenance.wateringNotes: No supplemental water once established. Thrives on neglect.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Can be cut back hard in fall to rejuvenate. Short-lived (3–5 years) but self-seeds prolifically.
maintenance.specialNotes: Nitrogen-fixer — improves soil for companion plants. Pioneer species ideal for establishing new native gardens. Fire-follower.

seed.description: Small legume pods; seeds persist in soil bank

phenology.ecologicalValue: Critical nitrogen-fixer for ecosystem health. Host plant for Acmon Blue, Orange Sulphur, and Gray Hairstreak butterflies. Abundant nectar for native bees.

wildlife.notes: Primary larval host plant; caterpillars feed on flowers and seed pods

image.attribution: Photo by Bill Bouton, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Named for deer's preference for browsing the foliage

image.attribution: Photo by James Bailey, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A showy spring wildflower that carpets hillsides with spikes of deep purple-blue flowers. As an annual, Arroyo Lupine completes its entire life cycle in one season, germinating with fall rains and blooming spectacularly in spring. Like Deerweed, it fixes nitrogen and enriches soil for perennial natives.

image.attribution: Photo by NatureShutterbug, CC BY 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Flat to gentle slopes; natural in washes and roadsides
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; tolerates clay; nitrogen-fixer needs no amendments

maintenance.wateringNotes: Supplemental water during germination (fall) and growth (winter) helps establish dense stands. No water needed once blooming.
maintenance.pruningNotes: None needed — annual dies back naturally. Leave dried plants in place to drop seeds.
maintenance.specialNotes: Sow seeds in late fall after first rains. Scarify seeds with sandpaper for better germination. Self-seeds well once established.

seed.description: Legume pods pop open explosively, flinging seeds several feet

phenology.ecologicalValue: Spring nectar for bumblebees and native bees. Host plant for several blue butterfly species. Nitrogen-fixer improves soil for companion plants.

wildlife.notes: Heavy-bodied bumblebees are the primary pollinators; they buzz-pollinate the keel flowers

image.attribution: Photo by Ken-ichi Ueda, CC BY-NC 4.0

wildlife.notes: Frequent spring visitor during migration events

image.attribution: Photo by Anne Toal, CC BY 4.0

wildlife.notes: Lupines are a primary host plant for this small blue butterfly

image.attribution: Photo by Bill Bouton, CC BY-NC 4.0

description: A fast-growing evergreen aster-family shrub native to coastal Southern California, with bright yellow sunflower-like blooms from winter through summer and glossy, toothed leaves. Sometimes called tornleaf goldeneye, it thrives on dry slopes and sandy, well-drained soils with very little water once established, and mixes well with other coastal sage scrub and chaparral companions Its long bloom season makes it a valuable nectar and pollen source for native bees across multiple families including sweat bees and leafcutter bees.

image.attribution: Photo by Nisdao, CC BY-NC 4.0

plantingRequirements.slopeRequirements: Excellent on dry slopes, canyons, and mesas; mounding habit
plantingRequirements.soilRequirements: Sandy to loam; tolerates varied soils when drainage is good; pH roughly 6–8

maintenance.wateringNotes: Very low water; Calscape suggests at most monthly summer irrigation once established. Overwatering promotes weak growth.
maintenance.pruningNotes: Prune in fall after flowering to shape; deadhead spent blooms if desired, or leave for birds.
maintenance.specialNotes: Plant in clusters for hedgerow effect or mix with other low-water perennials. Evergreen in mild climates.

seed.description: Aster-family achenes in dried heads; finches and other birds often visit spent flowers

phenology.ecologicalValue: Nectar and pollen for native bees and butterflies; Calscape notes likely caterpillar hosts in the sunflower family. Dense foliage provides cover; spent heads can support seed-eating birds.

wildlife.notes: Common visitor where yellow composites are abundant in spring and summer

image.attribution: Photo by Anne Toal, CC BY 4.0

wildlife.notes: Visits yellow composite flowers for nectar alongside insects

image.attribution: Photo by Greg Lasley, CC BY-NC 4.0
